definition.canonical_value: The software development lifecycle (SDLC) is a structured and iterative methodology used by development teams to build, deliver and maintain high-quality and cost-effective software systems.

alternative_values.value: The software development lifecycle (SDLC) is the cost-effective and time-efficient process that development teams use to design and build high-quality software. The goal of SDLC is to minimize project risks through forward planning so that software meets customer expectations during production and beyond.

key_properties.value: Each phase has its own objectives and deliverables
Forms a roadmap for software development
Deploy phase – moving builds to production with packaging, configuration, and installation
Design phase – analysis of requirements, solution selection, technology choices, and integration planning
Implementation phase – coding the product in manageable tasks
Maintain phase – bug fixes, issue resolution, performance monitoring, and continuous improvement
Plan phase – cost‑benefit analysis, scheduling, resource estimation, and allocation
Test phase – automation and manual testing for bugs and quality assurance

purpose.canonical_value: To provide a framework for managing and aligning factors such as stakeholder needs, resource availability, and the larger IT environment, facilitating a more streamlined development process, helping stakeholders estimate project costs and time frames, identifying potential challenges, addressing risk factors early, measuring development progress, enhancing documentation and transparency, and better aligning software projects with organizational goals.

alternative_values.value: To provide a systematic management framework with specific deliverables at every stage, minimizing project risks through forward planning so that software meets customer expectations.

benefits.value: Better aligns software projects with organizational goals.
Helps stakeholders estimate project costs and time frames.
Identifies potential challenges and addresses risk factors early in the development lifecycle.
Agile adapts to changes in user requirements faster
Agile gives higher priority to user satisfaction
Agile improves flexibility of software development
Agile makes it easier to make improvements or changes to the software
Improved risk management and cost estimation
Increased visibility of the development process for all stakeholders involved
Systematic software delivery and better customer satisfaction

definition.canonical_value: The software development lifecycle (SDLC) is a structured and iterative methodology used by development teams to build, deliver and maintain high-quality and cost-effective software systems.

alternative_values.value: The software development lifecycle (SDLC) is the cost-effective and time-efficient process that development teams use to design and build high-quality software.
The Waterfall model is one of the oldest and most straightforward approaches to software development, following a linear and sequential approach.

key_properties.value: Different SDLC models approach the phases in their own way; for example, the waterfall model completes phases sequentially, while agile models allow phases to be worked on in parallel.
Each phase of the SDLC has its own objectives and deliverables that guide the next phase.
The SDLC breaks down software development into distinct, repeatable, interdependent phases.
The phases together form a roadmap that helps development teams create software that meets stakeholder needs, project requirements and customer expectations.
Agile delivers a working prototype early and iteratively improves it
Agile methodologies are a collection of methodologies aiming to improve flexibility and adapt to changes in user requirements faster
Agile places less focus on documentation and gives more priority to user satisfaction
In Agile, design and analysis phases often occur together
In Agile, the problem is broken down into sections developed in parallel
Waterfall analysis stage includes a feasibility study using TELOS (Technical, Economic, Legal, Operational, Scheduling)
Waterfall model is based on a series of stages completed in sequence from start to finish
Waterfall model is inflexible; changes require revisiting all previous stages
Waterfall users have little input, involved only at analysis and evaluation stages
Phases: Requirements, Design, Development, Testing, Deployment, Maintenance

limitations.value: Little room for change once a phase is considered complete, as changes can affect delivery time, cost, and quality
Waterfall model is inflexible and unsuitable for projects with changing requirements
Waterfall model provides little user input, only at the beginning and end

purpose.canonical_value: The SDLC provides a framework for managing and aligning factors such as stakeholder needs, resource availability, and the larger IT environment, helping stakeholders estimate project costs and time frames, identify potential challenges, address risk factors early, measure development progress, enhance documentation and transparency, and better align software projects with organizational goals.

alternative_values.value: The goal of SDLC is to minimize project risks through forward planning so that software meets customer expectations during production and beyond.

benefits.value: Accelerates delivery of high-performance applications and services
Adapting to the market and competition
Allows earlier identification and remediation of problems through shift-left testing
Improving the mean time to recovery
Meets user demand for frequent, innovative features
Optimizes rapid delivery of high-quality software
Teams that adopt DevOps culture, practices, and tools become high-performing, building better products faster for greater customer satisfaction.
Automates build, test, and deployment processes to reduce errors
Continuous monitoring ensures availability and performance
Enables faster and more frequent software releases
Encourages close collaboration between development and operations teams
Improves system reliability, monitoring, and feedback loops
Issues are identified early, reducing cost and impact of failures
delivers applications faster and more reliably
reducing lead time for software delivery

definition.canonical_value: DevOps is a software development methodology that accelerates the delivery of high-performance applications and services by combining and automating the work of software development (Dev) and IT operations (Ops) teams.

alternative_values.value: DevOps is the combination of cultural philosophies, practices, and tools that increases an organization’s ability to deliver applications and services at high velocity: evolving and improving products at a faster pace than organizations using traditional software development and infrastructure management processes.
A compound of development (Dev) and operations (Ops), DevOps is the union of people, process, and technology to continually provide value to customers.
The DevOps Lifecycle is a continuous, looping process that bridges the gap between software development (Dev) and IT operations (Ops).
DevOps is a modern approach to software development that brings development and operations teams together to deliver applications faster and more reliably.

DevOps is a software engineering culture and practice that aims to unify software development and operations. It is an approach to software development that emphasizes collaboration, communication, and integration between software developers and IT operations.

key_properties.value: Iterative, automated workflows within a larger development lifecycle
Organizational culture shift fostering coordination and collaboration
Shared tools and practices between development and operations teams

purpose.canonical_value: DevOps is about meeting software users’ demands for frequent, innovative new features and uninterrupted performance and availability.

alternative_values.value: DevOps enables formerly siloed roles—development, IT operations, quality engineering, and security—to coordinate and collaborate to produce better, more reliable products.
DevOps integrates development and operations into a single, infinite loop of collaboration, automation, and feedback.
It focuses on collaboration, automation, and continuous improvement across the software lifecycle.

The goal of DevOps was to bring together software development and operations to work collaboratively, improving communication, collaboration, and integration. The focus was on automating manual processes, reducing lead times, and increasing the speed of delivery.

definition.canonical_value: DevOps is a software development methodology that accelerates the delivery of high-performance applications and services by combining and automating the work of software development (Dev) and IT operations (Ops) teams.

alternative_values.value: The software development lifecycle (SDLC) is a structured and iterative methodology used by development teams to build, deliver and maintain high-quality and cost-effective software systems.

DevOps is the combination of cultural philosophies, practices, and tools that increases an organization’s ability to deliver applications and services at high velocity: evolving and improving products at a faster pace than organizations using traditional software development and infrastructure management processes.
A compound of development (Dev) and operations (Ops), DevOps is the union of people, process, and technology to continually provide value to customers.

The systems development life cycle (SDLC), also referred to as the application development life-cycle, is a term used in systems engineering, information systems and software engineering to describe a process for planning, creating, testing, and deploying an information system.
The software development lifecycle (SDLC) is the cost-effective and time-efficient process that development teams use to design and build high-quality software.
SDLC is the process consisting of a series of planned activities to develop or alter the software products.
The DevOps Lifecycle is a continuous, looping process that bridges the gap between software development (Dev) and IT operations (Ops).
DevOps is a modern approach to software development that brings development and operations teams together to deliver applications faster and more reliably.
DevOps is a software engineering culture and practice that aims to unify software development and operations.
SDLC models are frameworks that guide the development process of software applications from initiation to deployment.

Software Development Life Cycle is a process followed for a software project, within a software organization. It consists of a detailed plan describing how to develop, maintain, replace and alter or enhance specific software.

collaboration and coordination between development and IT operations teams

System Testing phase tests the implemented system to remove bugs.
Each phase must be completed before the next phase can begin
Testing is carried out once the code has been fully developed

Problems with one phase are never solved completely during that phase
